claim: Primary cervical cancer screening with HPV detection has higher longitudinal sensitivity than conventional cytology to detect cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 2.

result.claim: Primary cervical cancer screening with HPV detection has higher longitudinal sensitivity than conventional cytology to detect cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 2.
result.reasoning: The claim is supported by evidence from multiple studies. Paper 27873158, a randomized controlled trial, found that for women aged 35-60 years, the relative detection of CIN2 by HPV testing versus cytology was 2.00 in the first round of screening (prop_205, prop_207). This indicates a higher initial sensitivity for HPV detection. Furthermore, the same paper concludes that "HPV-based screening is more effective than cytology in preventing invasive cervical cancer, by detecting persistent high-grade lesions earlier and providing a longer low-risk period" (abstract, prop_221, prop_222). This "longer low-risk period" suggests a higher longitudinal sensitivity. Paper 9764256 also supports this, stating that "Implementation of HPV DNA testing in cervical screening leads to earlier detection of clinically relevant CIN grade 2 or worse" (prop_987). While paper 27873158 showed a lower relative detection for CIN2 in the second round (0.54), the overall conclusion regarding a longer low-risk period and earlier detection of high-grade lesions supports the claim of higher longitudinal sensitivity. The studies are randomized controlled trials, which are high-quality evidence.

evidence.text: HPV screening provides a longer low-risk period than cytology.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: At the second screening 5 years later, HPV DNA and cytology co-testing was done in both groups.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: Implementation of HPV DNA testing in cervical screening leads to earlier detection of clinically relevant CIN grade 2 or worse.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: The relative detection (HPV vs cytology) was 2.08 for CIN3 among women aged 35-60 years during round one screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: Seven cases of invasive cervical cancer were detected in the HPV group during the first round of screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: HPV testing can be used for prediction of the therapeutic outcome after treatment of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia lesions.
evidence.source: Evidence regarding human papillomavirus testing in secondary prevention of cervical cancer.

evidence.text: The study assessed whether HPV DNA testing in the first screen decreases detection of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN) grade 3 or worse, CIN grade 2 or worse, and cervical cancer in the second screening.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: During phase two, women in the HPV group were referred for colposcopy if the HPV test was positive.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: Primary screening for hrHPV generally detects more CIN2, CIN3 or cancer compared to cytology at cut-off atypical squamous cells of undetermined significance (ASC-US) or LSIL.
evidence.source: Evidence regarding human papillomavirus testing in secondary prevention of cervical cancer.

evidence.text: HPV-based screening is more effective than cytology in preventing invasive cervical cancer.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: No cases of invasive cervical cancer were detected in the HPV group during the second round of screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: HPV testing is advantageous in surveillance after treatment of CIN lesions.
evidence.source: Evidence regarding human papillomavirus testing in secondary prevention of cervical cancer.

evidence.text: HPV testing is advantageous in triage of women with equivocal abnormal cytology.
evidence.source: Evidence regarding human papillomavirus testing in secondary prevention of cervical cancer.

evidence.text: Participants in the cervical screening programme in the Netherlands were randomly assigned to receive HPV DNA (GP5+/6+-PCR method) and cytology co-testing or cytology testing alone.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: HPV testing can be used for primary screening for cervical cancer and pre-cancer.
evidence.source: Evidence regarding human papillomavirus testing in secondary prevention of cervical cancer.

evidence.text: The relative detection (HPV vs cytology) was 2.03 for CIN2 and 3 together among women aged 35-60 years during round one screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: The relative detection (HPV vs cytology) was 2.00 for CIN2 among women aged 35-60 years during round one screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: HPV testing can be used for triage of women with equivocal or low-grade cytologic abnormalities.
evidence.source: Evidence regarding human papillomavirus testing in secondary prevention of cervical cancer.

evidence.text: Combined HPV and cytology screening provides a further small gain in sensitivity at the expense of a considerable loss in specificity if positive by either test is referred to colposcopy, in comparison with HPV testing only.
evidence.source: Evidence regarding human papillomavirus testing in secondary prevention of cervical cancer.

evidence.text: Nine cases of invasive cervical cancer were detected in the cytology group during the second round of screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.
